title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their approach to looking after their mental health. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Taking Care: Four People on Mental Health and Well-Being

texts.A:
### Katie, 34, Lawyer
[43]My mental health reached a crisis point four years ago when I realised I had been operating on adrenaline and caffeine for months and hadn't genuinely rested in longer than I could remember.[/43] I took two weeks off work – something I had resisted for years – and began working with a therapist. [47]Therapy gave me tools to recognise when I was slipping into a spiral of anxious thinking, and I now use those tools daily.[/47] I won't pretend the legal profession makes it easy to prioritise well-being, but I've accepted that I cannot do good work when I'm running on empty. [51]I now block out time in my calendar for non-negotiable rest the same way I would a court hearing.[/51]
texts.B:
### Thomas, 48, Secondary School Teacher
[44]I struggled with low mood for years before I finally acknowledged it openly to a GP, partly because I thought I should be able to handle everything by myself.[/44] That reluctance to ask for help cost me a great deal of time and suffering. The GP referred me to a group therapy programme, which I was initially sceptical about – sharing personal struggles with strangers felt deeply uncomfortable. [48]Over time, hearing others describe experiences that closely mirrored my own made me feel less isolated than I had in years.[/48] [52]I now advocate openly for mental health awareness in my school, particularly for male staff, because I know how much silence can cost.[/52]
texts.C:
### Amara, 26, Graphic Designer
[45]Working freelance means there are no set hours and no colleagues to notice if something is wrong, so I have had to become very intentional about looking after myself.[/45] I follow a strict morning routine – no phone for the first hour, a short walk, and ten minutes of journalling – which sets a stable foundation for the rest of the day. [49]I was resistant to the idea of a routine for a long time because I thought creativity required spontaneity, but I've found the opposite to be true.[/49] Having a predictable start to the day has actually freed up more mental energy for creative work, not less.
texts.D:
### Will, 55, Business Owner
[46]After selling the business I had spent twenty years building, I expected to feel relieved and happy, but instead I felt purposeless and hollow for the best part of a year.[/46] I had tied my identity so completely to the company that without it, I didn't know who I was. A close friend who had experienced something similar suggested I try volunteering, which gave me back a sense of contribution without the pressure of commercial targets. [50]I also started wild swimming with a group of men my age, and the combination of cold water and genuine conversation has done more for my mental state than anything else I've tried.[/50] I'm cautious now about letting any single thing define me entirely.

questions.question: realised they had been neglecting their well-being for an extended period before taking action?
questions.explanation: Katie reached a crisis point when she realised she hadn't genuinely rested in longer than she could remember, having operated on adrenaline for months.

questions.question: delayed seeking professional support because they believed they should cope alone?
questions.explanation: Thomas struggled for years before acknowledging the problem to a GP, partly because he thought he should handle everything by himself.

questions.question: has created a self-care structure specifically because their work provides no external support system?
questions.explanation: Amara works freelance with no set hours and no colleagues to notice problems, so she has had to be intentional about self-care.

questions.question: experienced an unexpected emotional low following what should have been a positive life event?
questions.explanation: Will expected to feel relieved after selling his business but instead felt purposeless and hollow for nearly a year.

questions.question: describes learning specific techniques to manage anxious or negative thoughts?
questions.explanation: Therapy gave Katie tools to recognise when she was slipping into a spiral of anxious thinking, which she uses daily.

questions.question: found unexpected comfort in discovering that others shared similar experiences?
questions.explanation: Thomas says hearing others in group therapy describe experiences that mirrored his own made him feel less isolated than he had in years.

questions.question: changed their mind about something they had previously believed would be harmful to their work?
questions.explanation: Amara was resistant to routines because she thought creativity needed spontaneity, but found the opposite to be true.

questions.question: mentions a physical activity and social interaction together as particularly beneficial for their well-being?
questions.explanation: Will says wild swimming combined with genuine conversation with the group has done more for his mental state than anything else.

questions.question: describes deliberately protecting personal time by treating it as a professional commitment?
questions.explanation: Katie blocks out time for non-negotiable rest in her calendar the same way she would schedule a court hearing.

questions.question: now tries to raise awareness of mental health issues among others in their professional environment?
questions.explanation: Thomas openly advocates for mental health awareness in his school, particularly for male staff, because he understands the cost of silence.
